source_metadata.title_raw: Jeffrey A. Walter - A-20-051 - March 26, 2020 - Sonoma

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
  
 
March 26, 2020 
 
 
Jeffery A. Walter 
City Attorney for Sonoma 
Walter & Pistole 
670 W. Napa, Suite F 
Sonoma, California 95476 
jwalter@walterpistole.com 
 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
 
Our File No. A-20-051 
 
Dear Mr. Walter: 
 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions change, then you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
 
QUESTION 
 
 
Do the Act’s conflict of interest provisions preclude you from providing assistance, advice 
and opinions to the City of Sonoma (the “City”) in connection with its review and decision-making 
concerning the proposals which it has received to establish and operate commercial cannabis 
businesses in the City given that you are a member and officer of the Board of Directors of the 
Sonoma Valley Education Foundation (“SVEF”)? 
 
CONCLUSION 
 
 
No. As a volunteer board member of a non-profit organization, you do not have a financial 
interest in the SVEF under the Act. Barring any other interest, the Act does not prohibit you from 
taking part in the decision.  
 
 
 
1  The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-20-051 
Page No. 2 
 
 
 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
 
 
You are the City Attorney for the City, a member of the Board of Directors of the SVEF, 
and you currently serve as the Board’s Secretary. You are not compensated for your service as a 
Board member. The SVEF is a California non-profit public benefit corporation formed and 
organized as such under the laws of the State of California. The SVEF’s mission is to accumulate 
funds through charitable contributions and the receipt of grant monies which are then donated by 
the SVEF to the Sonoma Valley Unified School District to pay for some of its educational 
programs. 
 
 
The City Council has adopted an ordinance permitting the establishment and operation of 
commercial cannabis businesses within the City’s limits in certain zoning districts. The ordinance 
and its rules and regulations create a procedure by which persons interested in establishing and 
operating such businesses can apply to the City for permission to operate such businesses in the 
City. In addition to going through a vetting process at the City Council level, applicants must also 
apply for and receive the relevant land use entitlement before being allowed to open and operate 
such businesses. 
 
 
The City is in the process of vetting the first set of cannabis business proposals it has 
received and you may be asked to give advice or other opinions regarding some or all of the 
proposals that have been submitted. One of the criteria that the proposals must address is the 
community benefits that each proposer intends to provide in exchange for being afforded the 
opportunity to establish and operate a commercial cannabis business in the City. Two of the 
proposers have indicated that they intend to partner with or provide financial assistance to SVEF. 
One proposer stated that it “commits to being a leading financial partner to the [SVEF]’s work to 
offer free early childhood education to Sonoma Valley’s most promising residents, our children in 
need.” The other proposer states that it “is in conversations with the [SVEF]. The goal is to create a 
partnership to help develop and start a program to provide funding and other supportive services in 
furtherance of the Sonoma Valley Unified School District’s effort to support the social and 
emotional needs of Sonoma Valley’s students and in the broader community.” 
 
ANALYSIS 
 
 
Under Section 87100, a public official may not make, participate in making, or use his or 
her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. A public official has a “financial interest” in a governmental decision, within the meaning 
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
one or more of the public official’s interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) 
 
Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest may arise and includes: 
 
• A business entity interest, where the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a).); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d).) 
 


File No. A-20-051 
Page No. 3 
 
 
• An interest in a source of income, aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value 
provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)  
 
• An interest in any donor, or any intermediary or agent for a donor, of a gift or gifts 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e). 
 
• 
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of 
his or her immediate family (Section 87103.) 
 
 
The definition of “business entity” under Section 82005 excludes non-profit organizations. 
SVEF is a non-profit public benefit corporation, which is not a “business entity” under Section 
87103(d). 
 
 
As a volunteer Board member for SVEF, you receive no compensation or reportable gifts in 
exchange for your services. Thus, SVEF is not an economic interest as a source of income under 
Sections 87103(c) and (e). 
 
 
Regulation 18702.5(a) provides: “A personal financial effect means the financial effect of a 
governmental decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family. 
The financial effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive 
a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision.” In this case, there are no facts to suggest 
that any decisions will have any financial effect on your personal finances or those of your 
immediate family. 
 
 
Accordingly, your volunteer service as a board member of SVEF would not be a financial 
interest within the meaning of Section 87103, nor would it give rise to a conflict of interest under 
the Act. 
 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Sincerely,  
 
 
Dave Bainbridge 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
General Counsel  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Zachary W. Norton 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
By: 
Zachary W. Norton   
 
 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
 
ZWN:aja

Sonoma Valley Education Foundation (“SVEF”)?

interest in the SVEF under the Act. Barring any other interest, the Act does not prohibit you from 
taking part in the decision.
